item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I started reading this scip and the more I read the less I liked it. I started asking questions like "How can a specific ritual keep popping up over and over again?" and "How the hell does the Foundation find these people?". Then I read further and most of these questions were answered. I came to the discussion with enough uncertainty about the scip to no vote. Then I was directed to the white text. That tipped it to a +1. I do agree with some of the above comments that this should be a Keter though as the Foundation has absolutely no way of containing this and can barely track down a few of the SCP-1304-1s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i dunno, this feels like 75% of a story without a satisfying ending, and i don't really get the context of the hidden messages. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding Keter class, but for different reasons.

Danger level is neither here nor there.
But the very first sentence points out that this ritual is uncontainable. The Foundation is, by definition of the effect, incapable of meaningfully containing the -1s either.

Not being able to even theoretically put it in a box is the essence of Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should this not be Keter? If someone wrote a character that ended the world it'd come true and the Foundation couldn't stop it after it was published.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. I wonder if this affects other people. If a real, existing person, separate from the instance and its family and all that, is mentioned in the book as dying or having died, will the real person die at that point in the instance's life? For example, maybe Joe McFictional becomes Vice President of the US under a real-life politician, and the plot involves Joe ascending to the presidency because of the real person's fictional death. Will said politician actually die? If said politician is very old when the character is born in the real world, will they be guaranteed to live until that point? Heck, if they're written as being younger than they actually are, what will happen? Will one of the politician's kids take their place in the narrative, like real cities stand in for invented locations?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very good, but should defnitively de keter, not euclid, both tge actual scp and it's -1 instances are nearly impossible to predict and impossible to contain

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, keep in mind that the concept of metafictionality, where the Foundation knows their world is sort of a story, with conflicting canons and retcons, is a pretty big thing in the SCP universe. My interpretation was that they wanted to see if the D-class got booted out into our universe, which would get them closer to affecting their canon from the outside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That makes me think it's something like writing between the lines in a book but it leaked somehow into "reality" (the reality inside that book, the Foundation's reality or ours?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one in general, but why the HELL would the Foundation carry out the ritual in real life? We know they're not above torturing people to death if there's a good reason, but "Let's see if this does anything" isn't a good reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That annoys me too, but it's part of an in-character statement rather than the official document, so maybe Head Researcher Appelbaum just isn't always very precise when he speaks.
Congrats on the two hundred (and first) +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just discovered this one thanks to SCP-2747. I'm extremely impressed. For one who's usually put off by length, this remained engaging throughout.

What I'm really impressed with is the idea that you're not actually performing the ritual described in the flesh, but writing the ritual, and that causes the effect. Add in the fact that the ritual was performed in the flesh in the SCP documentation, which, of course, only means it was written.

The hidden text leaves me nonplussed, however. I don't think that part worked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very well done, and easily gets a +1 from me. Gotta have to be on the lookout for more gems like this one. One minor complaint though:

it's statistically impossible for seventy-six such books to be published, even across the space of 500 years.

No it's not. The chance is really really really small, yes, but not impossible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't believe I missed this little gem before. +1.

item.title: Oh god...
item.content:encoded: Using this, you could transfer David Bowman into reality? Time to alter 2001…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Confusing (had to re-read a few parts) but VERY intriguing. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Correct me if I'm wrong, but does the hidden text mean that

+ show block
– hide block

the D-Class that SCP-1304 was used on was "translated" to our reality, since the Foundationverse is essentially one big story?


That's probably incorrect, but even if it is, I like that interpretation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really didn't like the hidden text, I felt it took away from otherwise a very, seriously-written article. I would have downvoted if I hadn't liked the article so much otherwise. Particularly liked the engineer becoming unremarkable when he joined management. The curse of us all…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have an odd habit of highlighting paragraphs when reading online. Just, y'know, double clicking the text to highlight the whole thing.

This SCP has made sure that habit will never, ever stop. Well done, especially the addendum at the end. Welcome to +100!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
that note from Dr. Applebaum reminds me of Stranger Than Fiction; in a good way.
Have an upvote
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really well done. For being so wordy it held my attention through its entirety and was never confusing or felt over-written. The collapsibles were all satisfying and felt like they belonged. Excellent job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know what? I've decided. This is my favorite SCP. It's richly detailed without being excessively so, is a wonderfully original concept, tells a compelling story, hides a bunch of clues in there you have to be paying attention to get, and reads like an actual documentation on an SCP object.

I can say without hyperbole that I love this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is awesome, and the "gimmick" is executed perfectly. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, I like this. I'm not actually a fan of the white text the way everyone else is. It might just be that I've seen a lot of it recently, or that I (think) I was the first to use it on the wiki, but I feel like the gimmick:help-the-article ratio is a little topheavy here. Also, I'd like to see a little more detail as to where the ritual comes into play in each book example. I had to scroll back to the top to remember what the actual SCP was since it was being referenced so sparsely.

Overall, I really do like this. The subtle implication that any one of us could simply stop being productive one day, just because a random author wrote us a different ending, is terrifying. The article's fairly long, but it comes together in a nice and creepy way that is definitely deserving of an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Description: SCP-1304 is a method of ritual murder which allows for the translation of a fictional character into reality.

And upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I won't lie. My first thought after reading this was to bring forth Batman. Then the Joker. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote from me! I didn't see the hidden text at first; that's why I tend to read discussion pages. I had already +1'd it anyway (as it stands strong without the hidden text too), but the hidden text makes me want to upvote this double.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's like this.

The D-Class had the ritual performed on them in the real world.

So they got sent to the world of fiction, and he's crying out in the only "story" about him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really, really love this one, but I don't see why there's a need for hidden messages.

What's being implied? I read the previous comments and still don't see what's so special about them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this, but could you please change "Edward Jan"'s last name to something else? Jan is just a Polish first name, not something that would be typically used as a last name. It's very jarring.

Also… since the SCP Foundation setting does contain anomalous objects, and the reincarnated character's life follows their fictional counterpart as closely as possible… wouldn't it mean that a character who, in the original work, dabbled with magic/superscience/aliens/etc. would inevitably come across a SCP or two in his new, real life?

item.title: The Ritual
item.content:encoded: I found the ritual to be slightly confusing. It's somewhat vague, which is ok. But the fact that it's uncontainable, with no apparent clue as to why it occurs at all is somewhat troubling. Then again the mystery is part of the fun. Overall I did find this to be an intriguing take on the standard fictional characters into reality thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Archetypes are more general, patterns of behavior, like "the child" or "the old man." This is a specific list of requirements. It's not unheard of for two authors on opposite sides of the world to come up with stories featuring similar characters fulfilling similar roles (indeed, it happens all the time), but it's much more unlikely for two authors to simultaneously develop a highly detailed ritual with multiple steps. It would be more analogous to, say, two authors independently creating the character Jean-Luc Picard, complete with the same backstory, personality, and speech patterns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this, only one point I'd like nit-pick about.

"For starters, it's statistically unlikely for even two books with the same ritual to appear when the authors have had no contact nor read each others' books; it's statistically impossible for 7█ such books to be published, even across the space of 500 years."

Well….it sorta can, but the explanation is less of an explanation and more of a "here's what the phenomena is called" type of thing. Good old Archetype is the explanation. It's the thing that explains away how people who could have never met or seen each others works that are otherwise separated by thousands of miles or possibly decades can come up with pretty much the same idea and act on it.

item.title: Re: Tedly I love your work
item.content:encoded:
This means a lot, coming from the author of one of my very favorite SCPs. Thank you so much!

Also, I've got another article in the works on the forum. Would you be willing to take a look?

item.title: Tedly I love your work
item.content:encoded: Don't ever stop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just imagine the Foundation have a sophisticated text search working in a range of languages running inside Amazon's servers to find descriptions of the ritual. Shouldn't be that hard to create a priority list of books for people to check out, and the Foundation's created similar tech to understand SCP-592. And it's not like the books are infohazards or anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't really speak to other creepypasta stories or anything—the Foundation is the only repository of online writing I visit, and I've only seen this done on one other SCP. (No, I'm not going to tell you. Find it yourself.)

As for why it's formatted like that…well, I've got a headcanon, but the other ideas have been so excellent, I'd rather not give an "official" answer and spoil all the guessing. Ask yourself this, though: who says this person/being/entity has the same formatting interface that we do? Maybe this is as visible as they can make it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Added a couple bits based on TL333s and Tabbyclaw's notes. Thanks again, guys!*

*"Guys" is used as a gender-neutral term for a group of individuals. Feel free to alter to your own gender preferences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was ambivalent about this, until I read the hidden messages, and then I downvoted. It's a gimmick, for one, and a gimmick that's been used in a lot of other internet stories. Also, supposing this is a official document that was altered by…something, that something took the time to color the text white so it would have to be highlighted? It breaks my suspension of disbelief instantly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finding the highlighting was irritating on an iPad, but worth it once I figured out how to read it. Good, even without it, but that adds an extra chill.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…it's almost like the authors themselves don't matter. Of course, if they're not doing this deliberately, then they're being used…

So it's sorta like the Death of the Author, except the author literally isn't coming up with the work at all?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really think you need to add a little bit about these other experiments in order to "earn" the last experiment in which they actually perform the ritual. That's the one part about this article that really doesn't work for me; it feels off for the researchers to have jumped straight from "this ritual has anomalous effects when written down" to "let's perform it and see what happens" without alluding to experiments done with it in its written state.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I stated on the previous page, I really like this one. I do have a couple questions though:

1) Would an instance of SCP-1304-1 be born/produced if the ritual were applied to an already dead person, an animal, a mannequin, or some inanimate object that didn't even remotely resemble a human? Or does the ritual specifically require that it be applied to a living instance of Homo sapiens sapiens in the narrative?

2) Let's say one of these works featured some twins, clones, magically-produced evil doppelgangers, or coincidentally-similar-looking-and-acting-characters (e.g. Thomson and Thompson). Would both/all of the characters end up being born if just one got killed in the ritual, or would each character have to be individually subjected to the ritual?

3) If the ritual were described Bond-villain-style down to the last detail by one character who planned on subjecting a second character to it, but the second character was rescued from the ordeal, would that still "register" as SCP-1304?

4) What would happen if the ritual was written into some surreal, incomprehensible, Finnegans Wake-style work or a word salad poem? Would a SCP-1304-1 instance still be produced somehow, or would the sheer nonsense of the text cancel out the effect?

5) Depending on how the work was written, could a SCP-1304-1 instance potentially possess anomalous effects or abilities on par with those exhibited by an actual SCP? Or does the process of producing SCP-1304-1 instances automatically negate any and all supernatural aspects aside from paralleling the character in the text?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, I read that as "Head Researcher Applebloom" at first.

He finally got his cutie mark in trans-fictional meta-ethics.

First off, I would think that they could have a chance at containing this SCP by notifying publishing houses to be on the lookout for the ritual in their publications. Unless these books are slipping through with no one reading them — and admittedly, they wouldn't be able to catch every instance — the Foundation could come up with any number of plausible reasons, most readily that of plagiarism of past novels, to keep the books off the market.

I had yet another experiment log that talked about this a bit, but ultimately I felt it didn't do much for the article as a whole. I should at least include a sentence or two of it, though. Basically, the Foundation let a book get published, then pulped the entire print run before it could be read by anyone, but the kid got born anyway. It doesn't matter whether or not anybody reads the book, so long as it's published, and the Foundation doesn't have the resources needed to monitor every publishing house in the world.

Secondly, why don't they interview the living authors? It's suggested that there's no link between them, but there's got to be something, some reason why they decided on this particular series of events occurring in their novel.

True, and I may slip in a reference to this, too. Like Applebloom Applebaum says, it's almost like the authors themselves don't matter. Of course, if they're not doing this deliberately, then they're being used…

Thanks for the good notes!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just want to say that the hidden messages creeped me the FUCK out when I first realized what was being implied. Without them, this would just be okay, but the messages really make this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This just blew my mind.

SCP-1304 has now joined Metafiction as one of the best uses of, well, metafiction on the wiki.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, I read that as "Head Researcher Applebloom" at first.

This is great. And yet I have so many questions.

First off, I would think that they could have a chance at containing this SCP by notifying publishing houses to be on the lookout for the ritual in their publications. Unless these books are slipping through with no one reading them — and admittedly, they wouldn't be able to catch every instance — the Foundation could come up with any number of plausible reasons, most readily that of plagiarism of past novels, to keep the books off the market.

Secondly, why don't they interview the living authors? It's suggested that there's no link between them, but there's got to be something, some reason why they decided on this particular series of events occurring in their novel.

It's not like these ommissions hold the article back, to my surprise, but I do feel they were overlooked and should be addressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did try to tell you about this with my post at the top of the article's comments…I assume when the Foundation carried out the ritual he was inserted into their report. You wonder if that's where Fred comes from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this one myself, and I had already upvoted before I found out about the secret messages. Then I was all OH SHIIIIIIIIIIII-

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvote removed on the grounds that the hidden messages are clever, even if the rest of this does nothing for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Originally the Foundation locked up every -1 instance they could, once they had passed their "narrative death," but that seemed overly draconian. After all, in most cases these are just regular people, without any world-shaking abilities or knowledges. We did lock up the serial killer, and five more who were probably guilty of similar things, but the majority of the "characters" are pretty unremarkable.

Of course, you're the expert, so if you think they should be imprisoned, I can change it easily enough.

Also, the initial discovery is mentioned in one of the examples. It's kind of buried, and there's not too much information, but it's there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's the D-class we performed the ritual on, reborn somewhere outside our reality?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fantastic.

This SCP is criminally underviewed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enh, I dunno. Why don't we lock away the -1s? And how did we find these in the first place?It really does nothing for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was under the impression that either the messages are from a literary character that wants to return to its story, or that the reader's reality is the story and the messages are from its "real" self wishing to return to its original home.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That… Wow… I'm kind scared. But also a bit confused. Will have to reread this again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice. My headcanon is that it's whatever it is that's stuck on Neptune sending the message.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The messages did it for me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An interesting idea, and it kept my attention. Plus, the hidden messages were neat. +1

And, this message raised my karma. Being good does work!
